## [03:04 - 06:07]

Have a look through it. You may find useful opportunities, but you can safely ignore this tab for now. The next tab are the campaigns. So a campaign is the main building block of your AdWords account. Inside of an AdWords account, you can have multiple campaigns. And I'll talk about this a little more when we get to the module on campaigns and AdGroups, where we talk about the structure of Google AdWords. For now, just know that a campaign is that main building block. And if we go down to the next tab, the AdGroup tab, a campaign contains multiple AdGroups. Well, it can just contain one, but one or more AdGroups. And the AdGroup is the thing that holds your keywords and your ads. So a keyword, and again, we'll cover this in the next module, but a keyword is what you put into your AdWords account that says to Google, if someone's searching for something similar enough to this keyword, show them my ad. And here's an ad. You put those keywords in the ads in AdGroups. You put those AdGroups into campaigns, and you put the campaigns into your account. I'll show you a picture when we get to the campaigns module that hopefully reinforces that hierarchy and that structure. Let's keep moving down the side here. Our next tab is the Ads and Extensions tab. Extensions, I'll cover this in a later module as well, are additional bits of information, really, that you can add to your ads, which make your ads stand out more, which help the user see if they should be clicking on your ad or someone else's. Google wants you to use extensions. They ethically bribe you to use extensions. I'll talk about that in a later module, but these are where you find the extensions in your account. Again, we'll talk about this in the next module, but the Keywords tab, that's where you'll find all of the various types of keyword that live in your account. Keyword is just a word or a phrase. It can be multiple words in a keyword. That's something that you choose that lives inside your account. And if that keyword is similar enough to what the person is searching for on Google, then your ad has a chance to show. I'll talk about that in the module when we get to keywords. A search term is essentially just what the person typed into Google, called a search term or a search query. A keyword is what lives inside your AdWords account, is the thing that you choose whether it's there or not. And then obviously, you can see on this screen, you can see how much you're choosing to bid, which is the CPC, the cost per click. The max CPC is the maximum cost per click that you are prepared to give to Google if somebody were to click on your ad.

## [09:07 - 12:08]

can set down to a very, very, very small area. If you're in the States, you can go all the way down to zip code level and say to Google, only show my ads in these particular zip codes. I'm not going to go into excluded locations much here, but you can kind of as a safety measure, you can say to Google, do not show my ads in specific locations. But you get to choose. That's the important thing. You get to choose how much you're bidding on this particular keyword. In other words, how much you're going to spend if someone clicks. You get to choose the daily budget for this campaign. Again, the campaign is a collection of ad groups, which is a collection of keywords. You get to choose how much all of that is spending, and you get to choose exactly where those ads show. So you have all of the control. You're not going to melt a credit card when you start running Google AdWords if you follow the training, if you get it set up the right way. You even get to choose the time of day when your ads show. So if you want to turn ads off on the weekend or in the evening or early morning, you can. You can bid more for certain days. If you look towards the bottom of the screen here, you can see that this advertiser is choosing to increase their bid. That's what bid adjustment means there. Increase their bid by 10% on Mondays. And increase it by 1% on Thursdays. So again, you have complete control over when the ads show. And if we go down to Devices, you even get to choose the device that your ads show on. So this particular campaign is set up to only show ads on mobile phones. This is not very intuitive. This is Google's way of turning off computers and tablets. There's not an on-off switch. You have to change the bid. It's wonderful, isn't it? Change the bid to minus 100%. Which means bid nothing. So don't show ads. Not very intuitive, I know. But the reason they do that is because you can have this bid adjustment. You can go from minus 90% all the way up to plus 900% if you really wanted to. So you can say bid 10 times more on computers than the other stuff. If you really wanted, you're not going to do that. But you could. So if you wanted to turn ads off on mobiles and off on tablets, you'd put mobile to be at minus 100% and tablet to be minus 100% and just leave your ads running on computers. And the reality is this is a very powerful way so you can bid differently on different devices. Way beyond the scope of this particular training. But I just want you to know that that is an option and to understand that you can bid differently on different devices. And then skipping over the advanced stuff, obviously for now, I just lastly wanted to point out the change history option down at the bottom there. So if you ever think, oh, I don't know what I did last week, has that messed something up? You can come into change history and Google will tell you exactly what you changed. You can filter this just by clicking within that red box there that I've drawn around that area on the screen.

In order to get your ads running, you need to give Google your credit card and you do that underneath billing and payments. The way it works, roughly speaking, is Google a cruise across, so you get clicks, you get clicks, you get clicks, and then Google comes along and says, right, now I need to take 500 bucks off your credit card. Thanks very much. And keeps the ads running, assuming that you pay. And that's all automatic. And then you get more, more, more clicks. Google says, right, you've run up a tab of 500 bucks. Let me take 500 bucks off your card. And then once you've built up a little bit of trust with Google, it'll do that every $1,000 or so. You can do invoicing once you're spending, generally the limit's about, you'll be spending about five grand a month for about three months and then you can move to invoicing, which means you don't have to pay Google until 45 days after the end of the month.
